subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Completed some college coursework in Mechanical Engineering Technology, but did not earn a degree
Received hands-on technical training during onboarding at the manufacturing factory
Participated in safety and machine calibration workshops provided by the employer
No formal industry certifications yet, but considering pursuing them in the future

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Mechanical Engineering Technician
Primary role is to operate and calibrate manufacturing presses for airplane parts
Responsible for daily production reports and quality control documentation
Involved in both physical manufacturing tasks and digital reporting duties

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the aerospace manufacturing sector
Factory specializes in producing parts for commercial and private airplanes
Role is firmly within the manufacturing industry, focused on mechanical processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized manufacturing facility with around 150-200 employees
Factory environment requires adherence to strict safety protocols (gloves, glasses, etc.)
Work shifts are structured and task-oriented, with a mix of individual and team activities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business focuses on supplying precision airplane parts to the aviation industry
Market segment includes both OEM (original equipment manufacturer) and aftermarket airplane components
Client base primarily consists of aerospace companies and airplane manufacturers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a Mechanical Engineering Technician
Started working at the current factory shortly after completing some college
Current role is the first professional position in the aerospace manufacturing field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an entry-level to junior technician position
Works under the supervision of more experienced technicians and engineers
Responsible for executing assigned tasks rather than leading teams or projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Begins each shift with paperwork and machine log entries
Performs machine calibration 2-4 times per shift to ensure accuracy
Operates a manufacturing press to produce airplane parts for several hours per shift
Completes end-of-shift reports and creates graphs summarizing production data
Maintains adherence to safety protocols throughout all tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: About 10% of shift spent on paperwork and logging machine numbers
Roughly 30% of shift dedicated to machine calibration (multiple sessions)
Around 50% of time spent actively manufacturing parts (operating press, handling materials)
Final 10% focused on shutdown procedures, recalibration, and reporting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Makes independent decisions on machine calibration and minor troubleshooting
Follows established protocols for most tasks, with limited ability to deviate
Escalates complex technical issues to senior technicians or supervisors
Has autonomy over daily workflow for assigned machine, but not broader production planning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Routine paperwork and logging machine numbers at the start of each shift
Operating the press machine after proper calibration has been completed
Packing finished airplane parts into boxes according to standard procedure

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Calibrating the manufacturing machine, especially when issues arise
Troubleshooting unexpected errors or inconsistencies during production
Creating detailed graphs and accurate reports at the end of the shift

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Manual entry of machine numbers and production data into reports
Repetitive quality checks and data logging throughout the shift
Generating routine graphs and summaries from collected data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys hands-on operation of the press and seeing tangible production results
Finds machine calibration satisfying when it goes smoothly
Considers repetitive paperwork and data entry the most tedious part of the job
End-of-shift reporting can feel monotonous, especially when rushed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry increases risk of mistakes and slows down workflow
Machine calibration can take longer than expected if issues arise
Switching between physical tasks and computer-based reporting disrupts focus
Lack of integration between factory machines and reporting software adds extra steps

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive and skilled during hands-on machine operation
Higher skill in calibration leads to fewer production errors and greater efficiency
Enjoyment is highest when tasks run smoothly and visible progress is made
Tedious or repetitive tasks tend to lower motivation and reduce perceived productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open to learning new tools if they make work easier
Initially cautious about new tech, but becomes more comfortable with practice
Motivated to adopt technologies that streamline repetitive tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts fairly quickly to software updates and new digital tools
Needs some initial guidance but becomes proficient with regular use
Comfortable experimenting with new features in familiar systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to spend time in short training sessions during work hours
Prefers hands-on, task-based training over theoretical instruction
Sees value in learning tech that directly impacts daily workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to save time and reduce manual effort
Heavy workloads can limit time available for training
Less interested in tech that feels unrelated to core responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers sharing positive experiences with new tools encourages adoption
Management support and clear expectations make it easier to try new tech
More likely to use tools that are widely adopted by the team

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses company-provided production management software for data entry and reporting
Utilizes ChatGPT weekly for report writing and calculations
Relies on spreadsheet tools (e.g., Microsoft Excel) to create graphs and track numbers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Operates a mechanical press designed for making airplane parts
Uses safety equipment daily, including gloves and protective glasses
Relies on physical logbooks and forms for some paperwork

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Basic familiarity with digital manufacturing management systems
Understands standard safety monitoring devices and calibration tools
Aware of common reporting and documentation practices in manufacturing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Company software does not fully integrate with AI tools, requiring manual data transfer
Graphs and reports sometimes need to be recreated in different formats for managers
Occasional compatibility issues between machine output data and reporting systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Has a basic understanding of what artificial intelligence is
Knows that LLMs (like ChatGPT) are AI systems that can process language
Familiar with terms like automation, algorithm, and machine learning at a high level
Not deeply versed in technical AI/ML concepts or programming

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT for drafting and editing production reports
Has experimented with AI-powered features in spreadsheet software (e.g., Excel's Copilot)
No use of specialized AI platforms beyond language models in daily workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses ChatGPT at least once a week to help word reports and summarize data
Occasionally uses AI to check calculations or suggest ways to visualize production data
AI tools are not yet integrated into real-time manufacturing processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI has helped reduce the time spent rewriting reports for clarity and professionalism
Once received a graph suggestion from AI that didn't match the data format needed for management
Learned to always verify AI-generated calculations and data before submitting reports
Found that AI is most helpful for routine language tasks, less so for technical troubleshooting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Received a brief introduction to ChatGPT from a supervisor
Peers occasionally share AI tips and best practices informally
No formal, ongoing company-wide training on AI tools as of now

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers to use AI tools independently for quick tasks like editing reports
Occasionally uses step-by-step prompts when unsure about complex requests
Appreciates the flexibility to choose between modes depending on the task

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as a helpful assistant, especially for writing and calculations
Does not see AI as a replacement for technical judgment or machine operation
Uses AI as a second opinion rather than a decision-maker

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to using AI more if it proves reliable and saves time
Willing to experiment with new AI features as they are introduced
Prefers gradual integration of AI into workflow rather than abrupt changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational AI interfaces easier to use for drafting and editing text
Rarely uses command-based AI interfaces, except for simple calculations
Prefers natural language prompts over complex scripting or coding

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Machine calibration and troubleshooting require hands-on experience and practical know-how
Production quality assessments depend on visual and tactile inspection skills
Paperwork and reporting are more standardized and can be supported by AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine language tasks and basic calculations
Always double-checks AI-generated numbers and summaries before submission
Does not rely on AI for technical or safety-critical decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive paperwork and initial drafts of reports
Believes human oversight is essential for machine calibration and safety checks
Values human judgment for troubleshooting and quality assurance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts automation for non-critical, repetitive tasks where errors have low impact
Finds high automation concerning if it reduces ability to catch mistakes in production
Wants manual override options for AI-driven processes that affect product quality

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Sees AI as enhancing efficiency in reporting and documentation
Believes hands-on technical skills remain essential for machine operation
Thinks communication and digital literacy are becoming more important

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to prompt AI systems effectively for specific work tasks
Becoming responsible for verifying and editing AI-generated content
Developing basic data analysis skills to interpret AI-assisted graphs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned that repetitive paperwork may become fully automated
Does not feel core technical or machine operation tasks are at risk from AI
Sees potential for AI to change, but not eliminate, technician roles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated reports are manually reviewed before submission
Human sign-off is still required for production data and quality checks
No fully automated decision-making in any critical workflow areas

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has recently encouraged more frequent use of AI tools for reporting
No formal policy on AI adoption yet; practices are evolving informally
Team is gradually becoming more open to digital and AI-assisted workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will increase efficiency across many industries, including manufacturing
Thinks widespread AI adoption will change the nature of many entry-level jobs
Sees potential for both positive impacts (e.g., safety, productivity) and disruptions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports responsible use of AI with human oversight
Worried about potential job losses if AI replaces too many manual tasks
Believes transparency and accountability are important for AI adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would like AI tools that automatically transfer data from machines to reports
Hopes for AI assistants that can spot and flag production anomalies in real time
Wants better integration between company software and AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects to spend less time on repetitive paperwork as AI automates more reporting
Anticipates learning new digital skills to work alongside more advanced AI systems
Believes hands-on machine operation and troubleshooting will remain important

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Provide regular training sessions on new AI tools and features
Develop clear guidelines for when and how to use AI in production tasks
Solicit feedback from frontline workers before rolling out major AI changes
Invest in better integration between existing systems and AI platforms
